Notice Of Motion And Notice In Support Of Motion To Vacate The August 19, 2008 Dismissal For Limited Purpose Of Entry Of Judgment Pursuant To Ccp Section 664.6 And The Stipulation Of The Parties; Declarations Of Jean M. Daly And Ledell Conner
Off Calendar Jul-17-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Plaintiff THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA, EX REL STATE, STATE FARM MUTUAL AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE COMPANY's Motion to Vacate the August 19, 2008, Dismissal for Limited Purpose of Entry of Judgment Pursuant to CCP Section 664.6 And the Stipulation of The Parties.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiffs' (People of the State of California ex rel. State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Company and State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Company) motion to vacate dismissal for limited purpose of entry of judgment is granted. "When there is a voluntary dismissal of an entire action, the court's jurisdiction over the parties and the subject matter terminates." (Wackeen v. Malis (2002) 97 Cal.App.4th 429, 437.) Plaintiffs ask this court to vacate the dismissal entered on August 19, 2008 and enter judgment against Defendants (Mobile Auto Glass Inc., Central Allied Glass dba Mobil Auto Glass of California, and Dale Williams) in the amount of $4,150,000 pursuant to CCP 664.6. Plaintiffs did not comply with all required procedures to ensure the court retained jurisdiction of this matter. CCP sec. 664.6 provides that "[i]f requested by the parties, the court may retain jurisdiction over the parties to enforce the settlement until performance in full of the terms of the settlement." This request must be made to the court. (Mesa RHF Partners, L.P. v. City of Los Angeles (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 913, 918.) Plaintiffs concede that they did not file a separate request to retain jurisdiction prior to the August 2008 dismissal. (Pl.'s P&A at 13:18.) The court has the inherent equity power to grant relief from a judgment procured by extrinsic mistake. (Moghaddam v. Bone (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 283, 290.) To set aside a judgment based on extrinsic fraud or extrinsic mistake, the moving party must satisfy three elements: (1) the defaulted party must demonstrate that it has a meritorious case; (2) the party seeking to set aside the judgment must articulate a satisfactory excuse for not presenting a defense to the original action; and (3) the moving party must demonstrate diligence in seeking to set aside the dismissal once it had been discovered. (Id. at 290-91.) Plaintiffs' mistake of law was reasonable. Plaintiffs' actions complied with the formalities articulated in Wackeen v. Malis. It was not until the Court of Appeal in Mesa, a decade after the dismissal, that these formalities included that the request for retention of jurisdiction be made to the court rather than merely be included in the settlement agreement. This is a departure from the lower threshold of formalities set in Khavarian Enterprises, Inc. v. Commline, Inc. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 310. There, the court concluded that "the dismissal did not deprive the court of jurisdiction to consider the fee and cost motions that were specifically contemplated by the settlement agreement, in which the parties specifically stated their intent to preserve the court's jurisdiction to consider those motions." (Id. at 326.) Further, Plaintiffs demonstrated diligence in seeking to set aside the dismissal. Plaintiffs only became aware that the court would not retain jurisdiction in February 2023. Therefore, extrinsic mistake relieves Plaintiffs from the dismissal entered in 2008. Plaintiffs will have to file a separate motion to enforce the settlement. (See Edmon & Karnow, CALIFORNIA PRACTICE GUIDE: CIVIL PROCEDURE BEFORE TRIAL 11:34.12 (Rutter 2023).)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/RBU)
